focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

The offices upstairs had translated the damage into a memorandum. It called the result a change in supervisory circumstances.

Leela read the phrase three times because the first two had not felt insulting enough. The memo sat open on her laptop beside the stripped-down dissertation, both windows full of tracked changes and institutional blue headers, both pretending language could make scale disappear. Change in supervisory circumstances. As if Mohit had taken a sabbatical. As if he had not spent a year turning his students into weapons against one another, then funneling their work into a startup deck with his soft little mentor's smile still warm on it.

Her studio was hot in the stale, boxed-in way graduate housing got hot once the radiators finally shut up and the building remembered it had no idea what to do with summer. The cheap blinds clicked against the frame. A fan pushed the air from one side of the room to the other without improving it. On the mattress behind her sat three stacks of papers she kept meaning to sort: committee forms, credit-card statements, and dissertation drafts thick enough to show what had been cut. One still carried the benchmark chapter whose figures had resurfaced in `ruderal_pitch_v3.pdf`. Another still held the robustness section that had looked gorgeous in a conference template and collapsed when she tried to make it behave outside the template. Mohit's phrasing still clung to the margins and transitions like oil in grout. The live file on her screen was one hundred sixty-eight pages if she counted the bibliography and the appendix she no longer respected. In January it had been two hundred forty-two. What remained was individually attributable work, which was the department's phrase for the part of the wreckage they...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...string down in tight block print that made every letter look like it could testify. Leela watched her do it and felt the useless appeal finally go dead in her. She had spent months trying to preserve a version of herself that might still deserve uncomplicated sympathy if the facts ever became public. That version did not exist. It had not existed the night she hid the logs, or the deadline week she let the false result stand, or the hour she doctored Omar's letter because the clean procedure was a polite machine for immigration disaster. Wanting a clean alibi had been one more way of staying loyal to the institution's taste in victims.

Marisol labeled a folder HOLDOUT SET / L.K. / MARCH 2026 and slid it into a drawer. "Leela," she said, looking up for the first time in several minutes, "they can already call you compromised. He already did. The committee already stamped it. The difference is whether compromised means documented and contextualized or whether it means whatever version he tells first."

When Leela stood, the room tilted from hunger. Marisol pointed to a basket of granola bars without looking up from her notes. "Take one. Counsel's not your mother, but counsel can still tell when somebody hasn't eaten."

Leela took one because refusing would have felt theatrical. It was peanut butter and stale at the edges. By the time she got downstairs the snow had started again, not dramatic flakes, just the dry mean kind that moved sideways under the streetlights. Her backpack was lighter by almost nothing. Upstairs, Marisol had the checksum on paper, the manifest in plain English, and the copied drive already in a labeled drawer.

Whatever story Haviland later told, `holdout_set` was no longer just a folder Leela could open, rename, or thin out at two in the morning.
